1: Tov shem mi-shemen tov — 'A good name is better than fine perfume.' The wordplay is precise in Hebrew: shem (name) and shemen (oil/perfume) are near-homophones that differ by one letter. The comparison inverts commercial value: expensive oil (shemen tov) is materially costly, but a good reputation (shem tov) surpasses it. The name/reputation (shem) is the intangible memorial that outlasts death; oil is consumed in use. Ve-yom ha-mavet mi-yom hivvaledo — 'and the day of death than the day of birth.' The provocative comparison: at death, the name is established and its verdict known; at birth, it is uncertain. The day of death is the completion of the narrative that birth only begins.
2: Tov lalekhet el-beit evel mi-lalekhet el-beit mishteh — 'It is better to go to a house of mourning than to a house of feasting.' The tov min comparison appears at the start of a series that runs through Ecclesiastes 7. Beit evel (house of mourning) vs beit mishteh (house of feasting/drinking) — the two poles of social gathering. Hu sof kol-ha'adam ve-ha-chai yiten el-libbo — 'for death is the end of every person, and the living should take it to heart.' The phrase yiten el-libbo (set/give it to his heart) means to internalize, to take seriously into one's deliberative center. The house of mourning is educationally superior because it confronts the visitor with the truth about mortality that the house of feasting conceals.
3: Tov ka'as mi-skhok — 'Sorrow is better than laughter.' The word ka'as (H3708, vexation/grief/sorrow) appeared at 1:18 and 5:17 for the pain that wisdom and anxiety produce; here it is the word for the productive grief of the mourning house. Ki-be-ro'a fanim yitav lev — 'for a sad face does the heart good.' The paradox: the face that shows sorrow (ro'a fanim, literally 'evil/bad face') benefits the heart. The lev (heart) that is educated by grief is better prepared for reality than the heart that has only experienced laughter's avoidance of it.
4: Lev chakhamim be-beit evel ve-lev kesilim be-beit simha — 'The heart of the wise is drawn to the house of mourning, but the heart of fools to the house of mirth.' The wisdom/foolishness distinction is made by what the heart is drawn toward — wisdom gravitates toward the instructive (mourning), folly toward the pleasant (mirth). Simha (joy) — the word used for God's positive gift throughout the book — here, when it is the fool's destination rather than God's gift, it marks the difference between joy as appropriate response and joy as avoidance strategy.
5: Tov lishmo'a ga'arat chakham mi-ish shome'a shir kesilim — 'It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise than to hear the song of fools.' The word ga'arat (H1606, rebuke/reproof) is the severe verbal correction that wisdom offers. It contrasts with shir kesilim (the song of fools) — pleasurable but vacuous. The same structure as Ecclesiastes 7:2's mourning/feasting contrast: what is unpleasant and instructive is better than what is pleasant and empty. The rebuke (ga'arah) is the verbal equivalent of the house of mourning.
6: Ki ke-qol ha-sirim tahat ha-sir ken skhok ha-kesil — 'For like the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of a fool.' The word sirim (thorns) and sir (pot) are another Hebrew near-homophone pair — the crackling sound of dry thorns as fuel and the pot they heat. Thorns are quick to light, loud in burning, and consumed rapidly without producing lasting heat or coals. The fool's laughter (skhok ha-kesil) has the same character: it is showy, brief, and leaves nothing useful behind. Gam-zeh havel — the verdict applied to foolish laughter specifically.
7: Ki ha'oshek yeholel chakham — 'Surely oppression makes a fool of a wise man.' The verb halal (piel: to make mad/drive to foolishness) — the same root as mehulal in Ecclesiastes 2:2 (madness of laughter). Oppression is not merely a social wrong; it corrupts the mind of the one subjected to it. U-yabbed mattanah et-halev — 'and a bribe corrupts the heart.' Abad (to destroy/ruin) in the piel means to make perish — the bribe does not merely bend the heart but destroys its capacity for wisdom. The two corrupting forces — oppression and bribery — represent external coercion and internal corruption.
8: Tov acharit davar me-reshito — 'The end of a matter is better than its beginning.' Acharit (H319, end/outcome/latter) vs reshit (beginning/first): the observation about temporal priority — what comes last is better than what comes first, because the end reveals what the beginning only promised. Tov erekh-ruach mi-gevah-ruach — 'and patience of spirit is better than pride of spirit.' Erekh-ruach (H750+H7307, long of spirit/slow to anger/patient) vs gevah-ruach (H1361+H7307, high of spirit/proud/arrogant). The same word ruach (spirit) describes both: patience is the spirit stretched out over time; pride is the spirit puffed up in the moment.
9: Al-temaher be-ruchakha likhtsos — 'Do not be hasty in your spirit to become angry.' Bahal (hasty/dismayed) reappears from Ecclesiastes 5:2; khas (H3707, to be angry/vexed) — the prompt anger that springs from pride (Ecclesiastes 7:8). Ki ka'as be-cheq kesilim yanuach — 'for anger rests in the lap of fools.' Ka'as (anger/vexation) — the word oscillates between productive grief (Ecclesiastes 7:3) and destructive anger (Ecclesiastes 7:9); the difference is contextual. Here it is the quick temper that lives permanently in the fool's lap — not an occasional visitor but a resident. The word yanuach (to rest/settle) ironically describes anger as having found its home in the fool.
10: Al-tomar mah hayah she-hayamim ha-rishonim hayu tovim me'elleh — 'Do not say, “Why were the former days better than these?“' The nostalgic question is identified as unwise. Ki lo me-chakhmah sha'alta al-zeh — 'for it is not from wisdom that you ask this.' The Teacher does not say the past was not better — he says that asking the question is itself an index of foolishness, not wisdom. Wisdom accepts the structure of appointed times (Ecclesiastes 3) and takes the present season as the context for action, not as something to be compared unfavorably with an idealized past.
11: Tovah chakhmah im-nachalah — 'Wisdom, like an inheritance, is a good thing.' Im-nachalah (with/like an inheritance) — the comparison is syntactically ambiguous: is wisdom better when combined with an inheritance, or is wisdom as good as an inheritance? The MKT reads it as a comparison: wisdom has the kind of value that a material inheritance has — a secure possession that benefits its holder over time. Ve-yoter le-ro'ei ha-shemesh — 'it benefits those who see the sun.' Yoter (H3148, advantage/profit) — related to yitron; another profit word. Wisdom provides a genuine advantage for those alive under the sun — not ultimate yitron, but real relative benefit.
12: Ki be-tzel ha-chakhmah be-tzel ha-kesef — 'For wisdom protects just as money protects.' Be-tzel (in the shade/shadow of) — protection from the sun, figuratively protection in general. Both wisdom and money offer shelter (tzel). Ve-yitron da'at ha-chakhmah techayyeh be'aleyha — 'but the advantage of knowledge is that wisdom preserves the life of its possessor.' The word techayyeh (gives life/preserves life) from chayah (to live) — wisdom has the quality of life-preservation that money alone does not. Yitron da'at (the advantage/profit of knowledge) — here a genuine, not merely rhetorical, yitron: within the under-sun frame, wisdom extends and preserves life in a way money cannot guarantee.
13: Re'eh et-ma'aseh ha'Elohim — 'Consider what God has done.' The imperative re'eh (see/consider) is the Teacher's characteristic call to empirical attention — but now directed not at accumulated goods or social structures, but at God's work specifically. Ki mi-yuchal letaqqen et asher ivvato — 'who can straighten what he has made crooked?' The verb taqan (to straighten) from Ecclesiastes 1:15: what was declared impossible for humans ('what is crooked cannot be made straight') is now attributed to God's making. God has made some things crooked — this is the theodicy realism of Ecclesiastes. The Teacher does not explain why; he simply identifies it as God's doing and counsels acceptance.
14: Be-yom tovah heyeh be-tovah — 'In good times, enjoy what you have.' The imperative heyeh (be) with the prepositional be-tovah (in goodness): do not spend good times worrying about bad times to come. U-ve-yom ra'ah re'eh — 'but in bad times consider this' — the same re'eh (observe/consider) as Ecclesiastes 7:13. Gam et-zeh le-umat-zeh asah ha'Elohim — 'God has made both the one and the other.' God is the maker of both good times and bad (cf. Isaiah 45:7: 'I form light and create darkness; I make peace and create calamity'). Al-divrat she-lo yimtza ha'adam acharav me'umah — 'so that no one can discover what the future holds.' The divine ordering of alternating good and bad serves an epistemological purpose: it prevents the human from predicting and controlling the future.
15: Et-hakol ra'iti bi-mei havli — 'In my futile days I have seen everything.' Bi-mei havli (in my days of vapor) — the Teacher's life itself is characterized as hevel. Yesh tzadiq oved be-tzidqo — 'a righteous man perishing in his righteousness.' Tzadiq (H6662, righteous) — the person whose life conforms to moral and covenant norms. Their perishing (aved) in their very righteousness inverts the retribution principle that wisdom normally affirms (Proverbs 11:19; Ecclesiastes 12:28). Ve-yesh rasha ma'arikh be-ra'ato — 'a wicked man living long in his wickedness.' Ma'arikh (lengthening, from arak) — the wicked person's life is extended, precisely when Proverbs says it should be cut short. The Teacher does not rationalize; he observes the empirical datum and lets it stand.
16: Al-tehe tzadiq harbeh — 'Do not be excessively righteous.' This verse is among the most debated in Ecclesiastes. The Teacher is not commending sin; he is warning against a particular kind of rigid, self-congratulatory righteousness that makes excessive claims. Ve-al-titchakkem yoter — 'and do not make yourself overly wise.' The reflexive titchakkem (making yourself wise) suggests performance of wisdom rather than its genuine possession. Lamah tishshomem — 'why should you ruin yourself?' — shamaim (to be desolate/ruined): the person who plays at extremes of righteousness destroys themselves. Verse 16 and Ecclesiastes 7:17 together frame a middle path: neither excessive moralism (Ecclesiastes 7:16) nor excessive wickedness (Ecclesiastes 7:17).
17: Al-tirsha harbeh ve-al-tehi sakal — 'Do not be excessively wicked, and do not be a fool.' The parallel with Ecclesiastes 7:16 is structural: just as excessive righteousness destroys, so does wickedness. Lamah tamut be-lo itecha — 'why die before your time?' The phrase be-lo itekha (before your time) — literally 'not in your time' — implies that death has its own appointed time (et), and premature death by wickedness violates that structure. Together Ecclesiastes 7:16-17 describe the two paths of destruction: one is the pretense of virtue carried to self-destructive excess; the other is unrestrained wickedness.
18: Tov asher tochaz be-zeh — 'It is good to grasp the one and not release the other.' Both the moderate righteousness of Ecclesiastes 7:16 and the avoidance of wickedness of Ecclesiastes 7:17 are to be held simultaneously. Ki-yere Elohim yetze et-kulam — 'for the one who fears God will avoid both extremes.' The yir'at Elohim (fear of God) is the practical navigational principle: reverence before God orients the person away from both the self-destruction of excessive moralism and the self-destruction of wickedness. The fear of God is the stable center between the two destructive poles.
19: Ha-chakhmah ta'oz la-chakham me'asarah shalitim — 'Wisdom strengthens the wise more than ten rulers in a city.' Ta'oz (H5810, to make strong/give strength) from azaz (strong) — wisdom as a source of strength surpassing political power. Asarah shalitim (ten rulers) — ten is a round number for totality; 'ten rulers in a city' describes comprehensive human authority. The comparison: wisdom exceeds the collective power of an entire city's governing body. This is a genuine yitron for wisdom within the under-sun frame.
20: Ki adam ein tzadiq ba-aretz asher ya'aseh-tov ve-lo yecheta — 'Indeed, there is no righteous person on earth who always does good and never sins.' The verse is the OT's clearest summary statement of universal human sinfulness as a wisdom observation. Ein tzadiq (no righteous person) — absolute negation. The Teacher does not say people are mostly bad; he says that the category of the perfectly consistent righteous person is empty. This verse becomes a foundation for Paul's extended argument in Romans 3:10-12 (citing Psalms 14:1-3 and related texts) that 'no one is righteous, no not one' — the NT reads this as the anthropological premise that makes the gospel necessary.
21: Gam le-khol-ha-devarim asher yedaberu al-titen libbekha — 'Do not pay attention to everything people say.' The practical counsel: selective hearing is wisdom. Not every comment, criticism, or opinion deserves to be taken to heart. Asher lo-tishma et-avdekha meqallelekha — 'or you may hear your own servant cursing you.' The specific danger of over-attending to speech: one hears things one would rather not know. Qalal (to curse/speak lightly of) — the servant's contemptuous speech about the master. The counsel is not to cultivate ignorance but to maintain equanimity rather than obsessively monitoring what others say.
22: Ki gam-pa'amim rabbot yede'a libbekha asher gam-atah qillalta acherim — 'For your own heart knows that you too have cursed others many times.' The counter-accusation is the corrective: the person outraged by their servant's cursing knows from their own experience that they have done the same. The lev (heart) that knows this is the seat of self-knowledge. The verse is a practical argument from moral parity: if you have done the same thing, your outrage at being its object is disproportionate. The wisdom is not cynicism but self-awareness.
23: Kol-zot nissiti ve-hakhmah — 'All this I have tested by wisdom.' Nisah (to test/try) — the same verb as 2:1 for the pleasure experiment. The Teacher has used wisdom as his instrument throughout. Amarti echekhemah — 'I said, “I will be wise.“' The aspiration is stated as past tense: he sought comprehensive wisdom. Ve-hi rachaqah mimmenni — 'but wisdom was far from me.' The confession of failure: after the entire Royal Experiment and the wisdom observations of Ecclesiastes 7, the Teacher acknowledges that full wisdom has eluded him. The wisdom that would resolve the hevel paradox is precisely what 'under the sun' existence cannot provide.
24: Rachoq mah-shehayah ve-amoq amoq mi yimtza'ennu — 'Whatever exists is far off and utterly deep — who can discover it?' The double superlative amoq amoq (deep, deep) indicates unfathomable depth. Mi yimtza'ennu (who can find it?) — the epistemological limit of all wisdom. The Teacher's conclusion at the midpoint of his extended investigation: reality is too deep for comprehensive knowledge within the 'under the sun' frame. This is not nihilism but honest admission of creaturely limitation.
25: Saboti ani ve-libbi lada'at ve-latour ve-vaqesh hokhmah ve-cheshbon — 'I turned my mind to know, to investigate, and to seek out wisdom and the explanation of things.' The four verbs (yada=to know, tur=to explore/scout, baqash=to seek, from cheshbon=account/explanation) accumulate to describe comprehensive intellectual effort. Ve-lada'at rish'at kesel ve-ha-sikhlet holalot — 'to know that wickedness is folly and that foolishness is madness.' The terminal definitions: wickedness reduces to folly; folly to madness. The three-level degradation: wickedness → folly → madness.
26: U-motze ani mar mi-mavet et-ha-ishah — 'And I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is a trap.' Mar mi-mavet (more bitter than death) — a comparative stronger than any other in the book: this is the most bitter thing the Teacher has found, exceeding even the worst of the hevel observations. The woman described here is not women-in-general; she is the Folly figure of Proverbs 5-7 — the seductress whose heart is a trap (metzodim, net/trap) and hands are chains. Tov lifnei ha'Elohim yimalet mimenah ve-chote yillaked bah — 'the one who pleases God escapes her, but the sinner is caught by her.' The escape/capture language mirrors the trap imagery: those who please God are not caught; the sinner is ensnared.
27: Re'eh zeh matzati amar Qohelet achat le-achat limtzo cheshbon — 'Here is what I found, says the Preacher, adding one thing to another to find the result.' The Teacher steps back from the investigation to describe his method: accumulating evidence (achat le-achat, one by one) to reach a cheshbon (account/reckoning/sum). Amar Qohelet — 'says Qohelet' — the third-person reference to the Teacher within his own speech is unusual; it may mark a moment of reflective distance, as if the Teacher observes himself in the act of investigation.
28: Asher od-biqshah nafshi ve-lo matzati — 'I have continually searched but not found.' The verb biqshah (seeking, H1245) used for persistent, ongoing search. Adam echad me-elef matzati ve-ishah be-khol-elleh lo matzati — 'one man in a thousand I have found, but not one woman among all these.' The verse has generated considerable controversy: it appears to express a misogynistic conclusion, but its context (the Woman-Folly figure of Ecclesiastes 7:26 and the Proverbs 1-9 background) suggests the Teacher is making an observation about the extreme rarity of wisdom rather than a sweeping judgment about women. In the wisdom tradition, the woman who fears YHWH is praised (Proverbs 31:30), but the temptress who is more bitter than death (Ecclesiastes 7:26) is a specific category. The one-in-a-thousand and zero-in-all-women figures are extreme rhetorical statements within the investigation.
29: Ledad ra'eh zeh matzati asher asah ha'Elohim et-ha'adam yashar — 'This alone I have found: God made human beings upright.' Yashar (H3477, upright/straight) — the created condition of humanity before the fall. The contrast is with me'uvat (crooked/bent) from Ecclesiastes 1:15: what God made straight, sin has bent. Ve-hemah biqqeshu chishevonot rabbim — 'but they have gone in search of many devices.' Chishevonot (H2810, devices/schemes/calculations) — the word is the plural of cheshbon: the Teacher's own investigative method is now used pejoratively for the human search for alternative designs to God's straight creation. The paradox: the human capacity for investigation (cheshbon) is precisely what has bent humanity away from the yashar state in which it was created.
